title: Blood Feud

jokes.text: Joke's on them. If the core explodes, there won't be any power to light that sign.
jokes.setup: Nuclear safety warning sign being dedicated
jokes.punchline: The sign won't work when you need it most
jokes.explanation: Classic Burns dark cynicism perfectly integrated with character. Brilliant logical observation that undermines the entire premise of nuclear safety theater.

jokes.text: He's never late. Something's wrong. Nobody leaves "Diamond Joe" Quimby holding the bag!
jokes.setup: Mayor concerned about Burns being late
jokes.punchline: Self-serving politician worried about his own image
jokes.explanation: Good character moment establishing Quimby's vanity and political instincts. The "Diamond Joe" nickname adds flavor.

jokes.text: No quack sawbones is going to apply his leeches to me.
jokes.punchline: Outdated medical terminology showing his age
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns characterization - his ancient worldview where doctors still use leeches. Great vocabulary choice.

jokes.text: He's suffering from what we call hypohemia. It's a lack of blood. Damn it, I know what it is! What can we do about it?
jokes.setup: Doctor explaining medical condition in simple terms
jokes.punchline: Burns irritated by the obvious explanation
jokes.explanation: Good impatient Burns moment. The medical obviousness works well with his character.

jokes.text: Skip it. Just leave me enough to get home.
jokes.punchline: Burns willing to risk infection to save time
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about medical safety. Burns' casual attitude toward his own health fits his character perfectly.

jokes.text: I'm B-positive. Damn this gutter blood! Smithers, don't feel so bad. After all, that kidney you donated to me really hit the spot.
jokes.setup: Smithers can't donate blood due to wrong type
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation of previous organ donation
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation revealing the extreme nature of Burns/Smithers relationship. The casual tone makes it funnier.

jokes.text: I'd give, except for one thing. What? I don't wanna.
jokes.punchline: Simple honesty instead of excuse
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection setup. The blunt honesty is refreshing and funny.

jokes.text: You don't wanna cash in? That's why you losers are stuck in this crummy job. I'm your supervisor. Sorry, sir.
jokes.setup: Supervisor criticizing workers for not being opportunistic
jokes.punchline: He's stuck in the same crummy job
jokes.explanation: Good workplace hierarchy joke. The supervisor's lack of self-awareness is the punchline.

jokes.text: Lemur. Lemur. Zebu. Zebu.
jokes.punchline: Awkward repetitive teaching method
jokes.explanation: Homer's well-meaning but awkward parenting. The repetition is mildly amusing but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: An ox with a hump and a dewlap. Hump and dewlap. Hump and dewlap.
jokes.setup: Lisa explains what a zebu is
jokes.punchline: Homer repeats the funny-sounding words
jokes.explanation: Callback to the teaching style. The words sound funny but it's a simple repetition gag.

jokes.text: My shoe size? 4-B. How many teeth do I have? Sixteen. Ring? Don't wear them. But three. Allergies? Butterscotch. And? Monster makeup.
jokes.punchline: Increasingly specific and odd details
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Marge's deep knowledge. The monster makeup allergy is a nice specific touch.

jokes.text: How many hairs on my head? Homie, you have lots of hair.
jokes.setup: Homer asking about number of hairs
jokes.punchline: Marge's diplomatic response to his obvious baldness
jokes.explanation: Sweet visual gag about Homer's baldness and Marge's loving diplomacy. Works on multiple levels.

jokes.text: I don't have to give blood. I have rights. Yeah, the right to remain silent.
jokes.punchline: Homer twisting Miranda rights
jokes.explanation: Clever twist on Miranda rights. Homer's parental authority meets legal terminology in a fun way.

jokes.text: You know 'Hercules and the Lion'? Is it a Bible story? Probably.
jokes.punchline: His complete uncertainty about its origin
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - confidently telling a story while having no idea about its source. The casual 'probably' is great.

jokes.text: How did a lion get rich? It was the olden days!
jokes.setup: Bart questioning the logic of Homer's story
jokes.punchline: Homer's completely nonsensical explanation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic from Homer. 'It was the olden days!' as an explanation for anything impossible is perfectly in character and highly quotable.

jokes.text: Otto-man! You work here? All my friends are in school... so I got a job as a bloodletting tech-dude!
jokes.punchline: His casual attitude toward medical procedure
jokes.explanation: Great Otto characterization - treating medical work like any other casual job. 'Bloodletting tech-dude' is perfect Otto speak.

jokes.text: Charles Montgomery Burns: American... and patriot. American patriot.
jokes.punchline: His grandiose but redundant self-description
jokes.explanation: Burns' ego on display even in his dying words. The correction shows his need for perfect grandiosity.

jokes.text: O, tyrant, hear his mighty name and quake! Smithers, I'm back! Top of the morning to ye!
jokes.punchline: Immediate recovery and cheerful greeting
jokes.explanation: Great tonal whiplash from dramatic death scene to chipper recovery. The contrast is perfectly executed.

jokes.text: Hey, there, Mr. Brown Shoes. How about that local sports team, huh?
jokes.setup: Burns trying to be folksy and relatable
jokes.punchline: Generic small talk that shows he has no idea how to connect
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns attempt at common man connection. The generic nature of 'local sports team' shows his complete disconnect from normal people.

jokes.text: I tried every tincture, poultice, tonic and medicine there is. And all I really needed was the blood of a young boy.
jokes.setup: Burns reflects on his medical treatment
jokes.punchline: Innocent medical procedure described in vampire/horror terms
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor making Burns sound like a vampire. The archaic medical terms followed by horror movie phrasing is perfectly crafted and highly quotable.

jokes.text: And all I really needed was the blood of a young boy.
jokes.punchline: Vampiric phrasing of medical procedure
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark line that makes Burns sound like a vampire. The casual delivery makes it even funnier and more quotable.

jokes.text: The son of Homer, a stiff in 7-G.
jokes.punchline: Dismissive description of Homer
jokes.explanation: Good Burns characterization - reducing Homer to a workplace identifier. Shows his detachment from employees.

jokes.text: D'oh! It's just a card!
jokes.setup: Homer opening what he expects to be riches
jokes.punchline: His signature frustrated exclamation at disappointment
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer 'D'oh!' moment. While not original, it's perfectly placed and iconic to the character.

jokes.text: Is that some way to show your gratitude? No gold, no diamonds. No rubies, not even a lousy card! Wait, there was a card. That's what got me so mad!
jokes.setup: Homer ranting about lack of reward
jokes.punchline: Realizing he contradicted himself mid-rant
jokes.explanation: Great Homer confusion moment. The self-correction shows his scattered thinking perfectly. Well-structured escalation and payoff.

jokes.text: You're living in a world of make-believe. With bells and magic frogs with funny hats.
jokes.punchline: Bizarre specific imagery of her fantasy world
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd Homer imagery. The specificity of 'magic frogs with funny hats' makes this highly memorable and quotable.

jokes.text: In case you can't tell, I'm being sarcastic. You stink!
jokes.punchline: Explicitly stating his sarcasm
jokes.explanation: Good Homer moment - he can't trust his sarcasm to be understood so he explains it. Meta but character-appropriate.

jokes.text: You are a senile, bucktoothed old mummy... with bony girl arms and you smell like.... An elephant's butt? An elephant's butt.
jokes.punchline: Bart providing the final crude comparison
jokes.explanation: Good escalating insults with Bart contributing the childish final touch. The collaboration works well.

jokes.text: You always do that hand thing. And it usually works.
jokes.setup: Homer giving in to Marge's request
jokes.punchline: Acknowledging her manipulation technique
jokes.explanation: Nice meta moment where Homer acknowledges the pattern of their relationship. Self-aware but not breaking character.

jokes.text: Think very carefully. Where did you see it last? The last place I saw it... was in my hand... as I was shoving it into the mailbox.
jokes.setup: Homer asks Bart to help remember where the letter is
jokes.punchline: Bart reveals he already mailed it
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - sets up like Bart will help find it, then reveals the problem is worse.

jokes.text: The last place I saw it... was in my hand... as I was shoving it into the mailbox.
jokes.setup: Search for the angry letter, with dramatic pause building suspense
jokes.punchline: Revelation that Bart already mailed it despite the family discussion
jokes.explanation: Excellent timing and structure with the pauses building to the revelation. Good plot advancement through character action.

jokes.text: D'oh! Why did you do that? There were things that had to be said. And I know you. There was no guarantee you'd still be mad this morning.
jokes.setup: Homer discovering Bart mailed the letter
jokes.punchline: Bart's logical explanation of Homer's fickleness
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Bart knows his father's temperamental nature. The logic is sound and funny.

jokes.text: I'll show you mad in the morning! Homer, you encouraged him. You should be strangling yourself!
jokes.punchline: Marge's suggestion he strangle himself
jokes.explanation: Funny escalation with Marge's dark suggestion. The logic of self-punishment for encouraging bad behavior works.

jokes.text: But don't other people have mail? So they won't get letters. You know the letters people write. 'Dear somebody... how is so-and-so? Blah, blah, blah. Yours truly, Some Bozo.' Big loss.
jokes.setup: Bart questioning Homer's mail destruction plan
jokes.punchline: Homer's dismissive view of all correspondence
jokes.explanation: Excellent Homer logic - dismissing all human correspondence as worthless. The generic letter format is perfectly observed.

jokes.text: God! Are you planning to water the mail? It wouldn't do any good to run because you know my name, right? That's right. Well, I'm still gonna run!
jokes.setup: Mailman caught Homer with the hose
jokes.punchline: Running despite knowing it's pointless
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist moment with Homer's illogical but instinctive response to being caught. The mailman's bewilderment adds to it.

jokes.text: Have you slept with anyone famous? Countess von Zeppelin and l-- What in blaze--?
jokes.punchline: Burns about to reveal scandalous detail before interruption
jokes.explanation: Good tease of Burns' romantic past. The Zeppelin reference fits his old-world character. Cut off at the right moment.

jokes.text: Listen to me, you bargain-basement Baudelaire! I'm not a starlet who can't string two words together. I can write this thing myself! You're fired!
jokes.punchline: Pretentious literary insult and dismissal
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Burns insult using high-brow literary reference. 'Bargain-basement Baudelaire' is perfectly pretentious and character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Hello. My name is Mr. Burns. I believe you have a letter for me. What's your first name? I don't know.
jokes.punchline: Doesn't know his own first name
jokes.explanation: Absolutely brilliant Burns moment - he's so removed from normal human interaction he doesn't know his own first name. Perfect character comedy.

jokes.text: Oh, look, Maggie! What is that? Dodecahedron! Dodecahedron!
jokes.punchline: Teaching baby complex geometry terms
jokes.explanation: Callback to earlier teaching style but escalated to complex math. Mildly amusing but predictable follow-up.

jokes.text: Give a fake name. Homer Simpson.
jokes.setup: Bart advising to give fake name
jokes.punchline: Homer gives his real name
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer obliviousness. Simple but effective character moment showing his inability to follow basic instructions.

jokes.text: I could crush him like an ant. But it would be easy. No, revenge is a dish best served cold. I'll bide my time until.... What the hell, I'll just crush him.
jokes.punchline: Abandoning sophistication for direct action
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion of revenge cliché. Burns' impatience overriding his pretentious planning is very funny and in character.

jokes.text: Senile, eh? Bucktoothed, am I? Bony arms, are they? Liver spots, did I? Shameless, will you?
jokes.setup: Burns reciting insults from the letter
jokes.punchline: Getting the grammar wrong on the last one
jokes.explanation: Good callback with escalating anger. The grammatical error on the last insult adds extra humor to his rage.

jokes.text: Now that's odd. I've just robbed a man of his livelihood and yet I feel empty. Tell you what, have him beaten to a pulp!
jokes.punchline: Deciding physical violence will help
jokes.explanation: Great dark Burns moment. His casual escalation to violence and treating it like a therapeutic solution is perfectly evil.

jokes.text: You can move in with your sisters and raise the kids and I'll... die in a gutter. It's practical and within our means.
jokes.setup: Homer planning their future after being fired
jokes.punchline: Matter-of-fact acceptance of dying in gutter
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor with Homer's practical approach to his own demise. The budget-conscious phrasing makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Beat him up. You got it. Wait. What is it? Nothing. Wait. What? Beat, but... don't kick. Got it. Wait. What?
jokes.punchline: Overly specific and contradictory directions
jokes.explanation: Good escalating confusion with Burns being weirdly specific about violence. Shows his disconnection from actual violence.

jokes.text: What did Homie do anyway? He saved Mr. Burns' life.
jokes.setup: Barney about to beat up Homer
jokes.punchline: Learning Homer is the guy who saved Burns
jokes.explanation: Good ironic moment setting up the moral conflict. Barney's realization creates the turning point.

jokes.text: I'm sorry. But I just couldn't hurt Homer. He saved you. I see.
jokes.punchline: Burns' understated 'I see' response to moral objection
jokes.explanation: Shows Smithers' moral line and Burns' casual acceptance of the betrayal - good character dynamics.

jokes.text: You've been the sober ying to my raging yang.
jokes.punchline: Mixing Eastern philosophy with his relationship dynamics
jokes.explanation: Nice Burns attempt at philosophy with his typical pompous twist. The yin-yang reference works well for their dynamic.

jokes.text: You've been the sober ying to my raging yang.
jokes.punchline: Mixing Eastern philosophy with his own dramatic language
jokes.explanation: Burns' pseudo-intellectual way of describing their relationship - perfectly pretentious.

jokes.text: Is Mike there? Last name, Rotch. Hold on, I'll check. Mike Rotch! Mike Rotch! Hey, has anybody seen Mike Rotch lately?
jokes.punchline: Moe unknowingly yelling crude pun to bar
jokes.explanation: Classic prank call format. While not original, it's executed well and became a series running gag. Good structure.

jokes.text: One of these days, I'm gonna catch you... and carve my name on your back with an ice pick.
jokes.punchline: Extremely violent threat to child prankster
jokes.explanation: Dark humor typical of Moe's character. The excessive violence threatened against a child is absurdly disproportionate.

jokes.text: One of these days, I'm gonna catch you... and carve my name on your back with an ice pick.
jokes.setup: Moe realizes he's been pranked again
jokes.punchline: Extremely violent threat over prank calls
jokes.explanation: Moe's disproportionate violence - perfectly in character for his hair-trigger temper.

jokes.text: We'll get the Simpsons an extravagant present. An unthinkable, utterly impossible present! A frabulous, grabulous, zip-zoop-zabulous present!
jokes.punchline: Making up increasingly silly words to describe it
jokes.explanation: Wonderful Burns wordplay escalation. The made-up superlatives show his excitement and pretentious vocabulary perfectly.

jokes.text: A pool table? I'm not going to turn his home into a saloon.
jokes.punchline: Burns' old-fashioned view of pool tables
jokes.explanation: Burns' antiquated morality - pool tables as symbols of vice from his era.

jokes.text: Don't you dare sully this moment with your price-taggery.
jokes.punchline: Burns' pretentious dismissal of practical concerns
jokes.explanation: Great Burns vocabulary moment. 'Price-taggery' is a perfect made-up word that sounds authentically Burns-like.

jokes.text: Have you beaten to a bloody pulp?
jokes.punchline: Casually referencing his earlier violent plans
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the beating plot. Burns' casual mention of violence as if it's normal is characteristic.

jokes.text: Wow! A crowbar! It's to open the crate, stupid.
jokes.punchline: Burns explaining it's just a tool
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with Bart's excitement over the wrong thing. Burns' blunt correction is in character.

jokes.text: Wow! A big, ugly head! D'oh!
jokes.setup: Family expecting riches in the crate
jokes.punchline: Bart's honest assessment and Homer's disappointment
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between expectation and reality. Bart's blunt honesty paired with Homer's classic disappointment works well.

jokes.text: Wow! A big, ugly head! D'oh!
jokes.setup: Opening the crate reveals the Olmec head
jokes.punchline: Homer's honest but tactless reaction
jokes.explanation: Homer's blunt honesty about the 'gift' - typical lack of social grace.

jokes.text: This gruesome customer is Xtapolapocetl, the god of war.
jokes.punchline: Unpronounceable Aztec name
jokes.explanation: Burns' scholarly knowledge mixed with the absurdly complex name - typical Simpsons fake erudition.

jokes.text: No, Maggie, not Aztec. Olmec. Olmec.
jokes.punchline: Lisa's perfectionist correction to a baby
jokes.explanation: Good Lisa characterization - her need for accuracy even when teaching a baby. Callback to earlier educational moments.

jokes.text: What does it do? Nothing. Really, what does it do? Whatever it does, it's doing it now.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to understand the gift's function
jokes.punchline: Burns' mystical non-answer
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist exchange. Burns' non-explanation paired with Homer's practical confusion creates good comedy.

jokes.text: I guess the moral is, no good deed goes unrewarded. Wait a minute. If I hadn't written that letter... we would've gotten nothing. Well.... The moral is, the squeaky wheel gets the grease. Perhaps there is no moral. Exactly! Just stuff that happened.
jokes.setup: Family trying to find moral of their experience
jokes.punchline: Giving up on finding meaning and accepting randomness
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary on sitcom moral lessons. The show acknowledging that sometimes there is no neat moral is sophisticated and funny.

jokes.text: Perhaps there is no moral. Exactly! Just stuff that happened.
jokes.setup: Lisa suggesting there might be no moral
jokes.punchline: Homer's enthusiastic embrace of meaninglessness
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-ending that subverts sitcom convention - sometimes there is no lesson, just random events. Homer's joy at this nihilism is perfect.

tmdb_overview: When Mr. Burns is saved by a transfusion of Bart's blood, Homer expects a handsome reward but is outraged to receive only a simple thank-you note.
insight: Season 2's blood feud climax delivers 63 jokes in 22 minutes, anchored by escalating family dynamics.
